title: Gunman Fires at White House Correspondents' Dinner; Trump Evacuated Unharmed
detail: At approximately 8:40 p.m. EDT, Cole Tomas Allen, 31, of Torrance, California, breached the main security checkpoint at the Washington Hilton and fired shots as the annual White House Correspondents' Association Dinner was underway — the first time Trump attended the event as president. Secret Service evacuated President Trump, First Lady Melania Trump, Vice President JD Vance, and cabinet members. One Secret Service officer was struck by a round but was protected by a bullet-resistant vest and is expected to fully recover. Allen was carrying a shotgun, a handgun, and knives. He was subdued by law enforcement and faces preliminary charges of using a firearm during a crime of violence and assault on a federal officer. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche stated investigators believe Allen was likely targeting Trump administration officials, 'including the President.' Trump later posted to Truth Social calling for construction of a secure ballroom on White House grounds.

media.caption: Security agents respond after shots fired at the White House Correspondents' Dinner, Washington Hilton, April 25, 2026

title: Judge Rules Trump Administration Violated First Amendment Over ICE-Tracking App Censorship
detail: Federal Judge Jorge L. Alonso of the Northern District of Illinois ruled that the Trump administration violated the First Amendment when former Attorney General Pam Bondi and former DHS Secretary Kristi Noem pressured Apple and Facebook to remove apps and groups that allowed users to track ICE activity. The court found the administration's demands — rather than mere requests — constituted unconstitutional government censorship under the Supreme Court's 2024 NRA v. Vullo precedent on government coercion. The ruling restores the 'ICE Sightings – Chicagoland' Facebook group and the 'Eyes Up' app by Kreisau Group LLC.

media.caption: Federal judge rules Trump administration violated First Amendment in ICE app censorship case

title: Board of Immigration Appeals Makes It Easier to Deport DACA Recipients
detail: The Justice Department's Board of Immigration Appeals issued a precedent decision making it easier to deport individuals with DACA (Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals) status. The ruling holds that being a DACA recipient alone is no longer sufficient basis for deportation relief — immigration courts may now more readily order removal for DACA holders. The decision represents a significant escalation in the Trump administration's efforts to end DACA protections, and comes one day after the DC Circuit struck down the administration's asylum ban at the southern border.

media.caption: Board of Immigration Appeals issues precedent making it easier to deport DACA recipients
